edge: The text-to-image generation algorithm is based on deep neural networks when text captions for images are unavailable during training.
Instead of generating pseudo-ground-truth sentences of training images using existing image captioning methods, the text-to-image generation algorithm employs a pretrained CLIP model.

node_2: embeddings of images and corresponding texts
edge: The CLIP model is capable of aligning embeddings of images and corresponding texts in a joint space, enabling proper alignment for zero-shot recognition tasks.
The text-to-image generation model is optimized by maximizing the data log-likelihood conditioned on pairs of image-text CLIP embeddings.

To better align data in the two domains, a principled way based on variational inference is employed, efficiently estimating an approximate posterior of the hidden text embedding given an image and its CLIP feature.
Experimental results validate that the proposed framework outperforms existing approaches by large margins under unsupervised and semi-supervised text-to-image generation settings.

The training objective in the first stage involves relaxing the KL divergence term to an adversarial training loss, specifically the Jensen-Shannon divergence, to allow more flexibility in choices for distributions.
The KL divergence is only tractable for a confined family of distributions, leading to the relaxation of this term to allow more flexible choices for the distributions.
The relaxation of the KL divergence term enables more flexible choices for conditional and prior distributions when considering text embeddings.

Text-to-image generative models have shown performance via learning with large-scale datasets composed of image-text pairs.
Existing algorithms represent each image with a sequence of discrete tokens and learn autoregressive or bidirectional transformers to generate high-resolution images given text inputs.
The introduction of diffusion models has paved the way to learn large-scale T2I models and generate high-quality images conditioned on text.
For training T2I models without captions, previous approaches typically exploit pretrained CLIP to approximate missing text captions.
CLIP-GEN assumes that a CLIP image embedding is perfectly aligned with the corresponding text embedding for T2I generation.
LAFITE adds a Gaussian random noise to the image embedding for estimating the unknown true text embedding.

Images without textual annotations are used by the model to generate high-quality images based on given captions. The presence or absence of textual annotations directly affects the quality of the generated images.

Conventional supervised training of T2I models utilizes both an image and its textual caption to estimate a conditional distribution, implying a direct relationship between T2I models and images in the training process.
T2I models are formulated as a task to derive the conditional distribution based on the input of a text sentence, suggesting a relationship between T2I models and textual captions.

Existing approaches for unsupervised training of T2I models estimate a latent text representation to derive the conditional distribution, indicating a relationship between T2I models and latent text representations.
An encoder approximates the text condition during training, implying a direct relation between encoders and the text condition input.

The CLIP model is designed to make image and text embeddings have a high cosine similarity, indicating a relation between the CLIP model and text representations.
CLIP-GEN approximates the text condition using the CLIP image embedding during training, suggesting a relationship between CLIP-GEN and the text condition.
LAFITE adds a perturbation to the CLIP image embedding to approximate the text condition, indicating a direct relation between LAFITE and the CLIP image embedding.

LAFITE achieves better T2I performance in terms of FID and IS than CLIP-GEN due to larger regions corresponding to noisy text features.
LAFITE is outperformed by VDL in semi-supervised learning scenarios on the MS-COCO dataset, showing large margins in terms of FID and IS.
VDL outperforms LAFITE even in the unsupervised setting with a ratio of 0.3, indicating more annotation-efficiency.
Comparison between KL divergence and JS divergence is presented in Table 3.
The proposed method is more annotation-efficient. This implies a relationship between the method and the process of annotation.

Comparison between the KL divergence and JS divergence is presented under the unsupervised setting on the MS-COCO dataset using StyleGAN2. This comparison indicates a relationship between the two types of divergences.

KLD optimizes with the assumption that the variational distribution follows the Gaussian distribution with Gaussian mixture models for the prior. This shows the relationship between optimization and the variational distribution.
DualKLD optimizes the dual representation of the KL objective. This relationship indicates how the DualKLD method is related to optimizing the KL objective.

When more text captions become available, VDL obtains higher IS, Simtxt, and Simimg while FID is unfortunately saturated at an early stage. This shows a relationship between text captions and the VDL metric.
In terms of Simtxt under semi-supervised settings, LAFITE outperforms VDL. This indicates a relationship between the Simtxt metric and the performance of the LAFITE method.

The strategy of training a learnable network on paired data by making the predicted text embeddings close to the true ones turns out to be ineffective for improving FID and even degrades the IS score. This relationship highlights the impact of the strategy on the FID metric.

We study the effect of using the JS divergence instead of KL divergence under the unsupervised setting with the StyleGAN2 architecture on MS-COCO. The comparison presented in Table 3 indicates a relationship between the two types of divergences.
